question: Producing aluminium from recycled aluminium uses roughly how much energy compared with producing it from virgin raw materials?

answers.explanation: Aluminium can be recycled endlessly without losing value. Using recycled aluminium requires less than 5% of the energy needed compared to aluminium from virgin materials — one of the strongest cases for material recycling.

Aluminium can be recycled endlessly without losing value. Using recycled aluminium requires less than 5% of the energy needed compared to aluminium from virgin materials — one of the strongest cases for material recycling.

question: How much lower is the climate impact of buying a second-hand garment compared to buying new (with comparable use)?

answers.explanation: The impact is larger than that. Research shows that within the same behaviour scenario, embracing second-hand consumption instead of buying new clothes leads to up to 42% lower impacts for climate change and cumulative energy demand, 42–53% for freshwater eutrophication, and 35–53% for water scarcity footprint per use.

Close, but the savings are even bigger. Research shows that within the same behaviour scenario, embracing second-hand consumption instead of buying new clothes leads to up to 42% lower impacts for climate change and cumulative energy demand, 42–53% for freshwater eutrophication, and 35–53% for water scarcity footprint per use.

Research shows that within the same behaviour scenario, embracing second-hand consumption instead of buying new clothes leads to up to 42% lower impacts for climate change and cumulative energy demand, 42–53% for freshwater eutrophication, and 35–53% for water scarcity footprint per use.

Not quite that high. Research shows that within the same behaviour scenario, embracing second-hand consumption instead of buying new clothes leads to up to 42% lower impacts for climate change and cumulative energy demand, 42–53% for freshwater eutrophication, and 35–53% for water scarcity footprint per use.

text: Recycling 10 plastic bottles saves enough energy to power a laptop for more than 25 hours.

question: Switching from a medium-meat diet to a vegetarian diet for a year can save roughly enough CO₂e to offset the production emissions of…

answers.explanation: The savings are much larger. Switching from a medium-meat diet to a vegetarian diet saves about 1,230 kg CO₂e per year — comparable to the production of more than 4 laptops.
Think bigger. Switching from a medium-meat diet to a vegetarian diet saves about 1,230 kg CO₂e per year — comparable to the production of more than 4 laptops.

Based on UK dietary emissions data, switching from a medium-meat diet to a vegetarian diet saves about 1,230 kg CO₂e per year. Depending on the laptop model, production emissions can be around 280 kg CO₂e, so that is comparable to the production of more than 4 laptops.

That would be great, but the actual savings are more modest. Switching from a medium-meat diet to a vegetarian diet saves about 1,230 kg CO₂e per year — comparable to the production of more than 4 laptops.

question: How much CO₂e do you save per year if you replace a 10 km daily round trip to the office by car with a regular bicycle?

answers.explanation: The savings are larger. A regular bicycle emits around 21 g CO₂e/km vs. 271 g CO₂e/km for a car. Over 250 working days and 10 km, that's approximately 625 kg CO₂e saved per year.
Higher than that. A regular bicycle emits around 21 g CO₂e/km vs. 271 g CO₂e/km for a car. Over 250 working days and 10 km, that's approximately 625 kg CO₂e saved per year.

According to the European Cyclists' Federation, a regular bicycle emits around 21 g CO₂e per passenger-km (including production, maintenance and food). A car emits around 271 g CO₂e per passenger-km. For a 10 km round trip over 250 working days: (271 − 21) × 10 × 250 = 625,000 g ≈ 625 kg CO₂e saved per year — about 92% less emissions than driving.
Not quite that high. The actual saving is approximately 625 kg CO₂e per year, based on the difference between car (271 g/km) and bicycle (21 g/km) emissions over 250 working days.

question: Why can a phone have a larger material footprint than its physical weight suggests?

answers.text: Because the packaging and logistics are not included
answers.explanation: Packaging and logistics are not the main factors. The Circularity Gap methodology uses Raw Material Consumption because products often require far more raw materials than their final weight suggests throughout the supply chain.

answers.text: Because raw materials used in extraction, processing and production are not visible in the final product
answers.explanation: The Circularity Gap methodology uses Raw Material Consumption because products often require far more raw materials than their final weight suggests; a smartphone weighs little, but requires many more raw materials throughout the supply chain.
Electronics can be recycled. The real reason is that products often require far more raw materials than their final weight suggests throughout extraction, processing and production.

Software itself doesn't create material waste. The key insight is that raw materials used in extraction, processing and production are not visible in the final product — a phone requires far more resources than its weight suggests.

answers.text: Recycling is one of the most circular strategies
answers.explanation: Recycling is important, but source reduction and reuse come before waste is created and typically have more impact than managing waste afterwards.

answers.text: Compostable single-use items are always circular
answers.explanation: Compostable items still represent single-use consumption. Avoiding unnecessary material use often has more impact than managing waste afterwards.

answers.text: Avoiding unnecessary material use often has more impact than managing waste afterwards
answers.explanation: EPA states that source reduction and reuse occur before waste is created and are among the most effective ways to conserve natural resources, protect the environment and save money. Recycling remains important, but only comes after reduce/reuse options are exhausted.

answers.text: Landfill is circular if the waste is sorted first
answers.explanation: Landfill is the opposite of circular — it's end-of-life disposal. Avoiding unnecessary material use and reuse have far more impact than managing waste afterwards.

question: How much CO₂ do you save per person if you travel from the Sia Paris office to the Sia Amsterdam office by train instead of by car?

answers.explanation: The savings are much larger. A Paris–Amsterdam journey emits around 3.0 kg CO₂ per person by train vs. 39.0 kg CO₂ by car — a saving of about 36 kg CO₂.
The actual saving is about double that. A Paris–Amsterdam journey emits around 3.0 kg CO₂ per person by train vs. 39.0 kg CO₂ by car — a saving of about 36 kg CO₂.

According to Eurostar's route-based carbon figures, a Paris–Amsterdam journey emits around 3.0 kg CO₂ per person by train, compared with around 39.0 kg CO₂ per person by car. That means the train saves approximately 36 kg CO₂ per person — about 92% less emissions than driving.
Not quite that high. Based on Eurostar's figures, the saving is approximately 36 kg CO₂ per person (3.0 kg by train vs. 39.0 kg by car).

question: You live in a 70 m² Amsterdam apartment with a gas boiler. Instead of keeping it warm during the 8 hours you're at work, you turn down the heating 5°C. Roughly how many kilometres could you drive an average petrol car and still come out CO₂-neutral compared to that heating saving?

answers.explanation: The saving is bigger than that. The avoided heating emits roughly 4–7 kg CO₂, and at ~0.19 kg CO₂/km for a petrol car, that works out to about 20–35 km of driving.

The saved heating emits roughly 4–7 kg CO₂, and an average petrol car emits around 0.18–0.20 kg CO₂ per km. So 4–7 kg ÷ 0.19 kg/km ≈ 20–35 km of driving. Note: reheating a cooled apartment takes a bit more energy than just maintaining it, so the real savings may be slightly less — maybe 15–30 km in practice. Still, a modest commute by car roughly cancels out the heating you saved.
Not quite that far. The avoided heating emits roughly 4–7 kg CO₂, which at ~0.19 kg CO₂/km equals about 20–35 km of driving.
That’s much too high. The saved heating emits roughly 4–7 kg CO₂, enough to offset only about 20–35 km of petrol car driving.

question: Of all the plastic ever produced in history, roughly how much has actually been recycled?

answers.explanation: According to the landmark 2017 study by Geyer, Jambeck & Law in Science Advances, of the 8.3 billion metric tons of plastic ever produced, only 9% has been recycled. About 12% was incinerated, and 79% accumulated in landfills or the natural environment. The OECD confirmed in 2022 that this 9% figure still holds. The Royal Statistical Society named it their “statistic of the year” in 2018.
It’s much lower than that. Research shows only about 9% of all plastic ever made has been recycled. 79% has ended up in landfills or the natural environment, and 12% was incinerated.

Unfortunately nowhere near that. Only about 9% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled, according to the foundational 2017 study by Geyer, Jambeck & Law — a figure confirmed by the OECD in 2022.
The reality is far lower. Only about 9% of all plastic ever made has been recycled. The vast majority — 79% — has accumulated in landfills, dumps or the natural environment.

question: If the whole world shifted to a plant-based diet, by roughly how much could global agricultural land use shrink, freeing space for forests and ecosystems to return?

answers.explanation: The potential is far greater. The landmark Poore & Nemecek (2018) meta-analysis in Science found that a global shift to a plant-based diet could cut agricultural land use by about 75% — from roughly 4 billion to 1 billion hectares — because most farmland currently grows pasture and animal feed rather than food for people.

Higher than that. Poore & Nemecek's 2018 Science study found a global plant-based diet could reduce agricultural land use by around 75%, since roughly 80% of farmland is tied up in meat and dairy production while delivering only a small fraction of calories.

The largest meta-analysis of global food systems to date — Poore & Nemecek (2018), published in Science, covering 38,700 farms across 119 countries — found that a global shift to a plant-based diet could shrink agricultural land use from about 4.1 billion to 1 billion hectares: a 75% reduction. That freed-up land — an area roughly the size of North America and Brazil combined — could return to forests, restored ecosystems and natural carbon sinks. Crucially, big reductions are possible even without going fully vegan; cutting red meat and dairy delivers most of the gain.

answers.text: Land use would stay about the same
answers.explanation: It would fall dramatically. Poore & Nemecek's 2018 Science meta-analysis found a global plant-based diet could cut agricultural land use by about 75% — from roughly 4 to 1 billion hectares — because animal agriculture is highly land-intensive relative to the calories it provides.
